skill.name: fp-ts-pragmatic
skill.description: A practical guide for using fp-ts in TypeScript with clear patterns for pipe, Option, Either, map, and flatMap.
skill.summary: Use fp-ts patterns pragmatically in readable TypeScript code.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backticks, fetch calls, and keyword matches, but the only file is a Markdown guide with TypeScript examples. I found no executable scripts, no command execution instructions, no data exfiltration behavior, and no prompt injection attempt.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives. The flagged backticks are Markdown code fences, TypeScript template literals, and inline documentation examples, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and these ranges are documentation snippets. No executable skill script or instruction to run shell commands is present.

low_findings.title: Network Warnings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The network findings are false positives. The source URL is metadata, and the fetch calls are relative API examples used to explain async code style.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch examples use relative paths and do not send secrets or collected data to an external host. The hardcoded URL is only a source reference.

low_findings.title: Keyword Matches Do Not Indicate Malware Behavior
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance warnings are false positives from prose and validation examples. The lines show descriptions and email checks, not crypto or system inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible documentation and TypeScript validation examples. I found no cryptographic API use, host probing, environment access, or filesystem enumeration.

content.value_statement: fp-ts can improve TypeScript reliability, but many teams find it hard to read. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for readable functional code.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use pipe, Option, Either, map, and flatMap in TypeScript.
Shows before-and-after refactors from imperative code to fp-ts patterns.
Provides plain-language translations for common fp-ts functions.
Highlights cases where simple TypeScript is better than functional abstractions.
Guides readable error modeling with Either and typed error objects.
limitations: It is a Markdown guide, not an executable tool or code generator.
It covers beginner and intermediate fp-ts patterns, not advanced category theory.
It does not verify project-specific type errors or runtime behavior.
It assumes the project already uses TypeScript and can install fp-ts.

use_cases.title: Refactor Nullable Logic
use_cases.description: Convert repeated null checks into readable Option flows when the team benefits from explicit missing-value handling.

use_cases.title: Make Expected Errors Explicit
use_cases.description: Replace thrown errors with Either results for parsing, validation, and business rules that callers must handle.

use_cases.title: Set Team Guidelines
use_cases.description: Define when fp-ts improves readability and when plain TypeScript is the better choice for a codebase.

prompt_templates.title: Explain an fp-ts Concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the fp-ts-pragmatic skill, explain Option in plain TypeScript terms. Include when to use it and when to avoid it.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Small Function
prompt_templates.scenario: Move one function to a clearer functional style
prompt_templates.prompt: Using this skill, refactor my nullable TypeScript function with Option. Keep the result readable for junior developers.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Error Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Replace expected exceptions with typed results
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the fp-ts-pragmatic approach, convert this validation flow from thrown errors to Either with clear error messages.

prompt_templates.title: Review fp-ts Usage
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of readability and maintainability
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this TypeScript module for overuse or underuse of fp-ts. Recommend changes that improve clarity without adding abstraction.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript function has nested null checks for user address fields.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains whether Option is useful, then describes a clearer flow with nullable wrapping, chained access, and a default value.

output_examples.input: A parser throws errors for invalid user input.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends returning explicit success or failure values, with simple error messages callers can handle directly.

output_examples.input: A team has dense fp-ts code that only one developer understands.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies over-composed sections and recommends simpler TypeScript or more direct pipe steps.

best_practices: Use fp-ts where it makes failure, absence, or transformation clearer to readers.
Prefer small, named functions before composing long pipe chains.
Keep simple TypeScript when optional chaining or direct loops are easier to understand.
anti_patterns: Do not replace every null check with Option when optional chaining is clearer.
Do not build dense point-free pipelines that hide business logic.
Do not introduce fp-ts patterns without team agreement and code review standards.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with TypeScript examples and does not include executable scripts.

faq.question: Is this skill only for advanced functional programmers?
faq.answer: No. It explains common fp-ts patterns in plain language for practical TypeScript work.

faq.question: Can it help with existing imperative code?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides before-and-after refactor patterns for null handling, validation, and async flows.

faq.question: Does it recommend using fp-ts everywhere?
faq.answer: No. It explicitly advises plain TypeScript when functional patterns reduce readability.

faq.question: Which fp-ts concepts are covered?
faq.answer: It focuses on pipe, Option, Either, map, flatMap, TaskEither, and typed error patterns.

faq.question: Does it replace TypeScript type checking?
faq.answer: No. Use normal project tooling to verify types, tests, and runtime behavior.
